summary: Justin Brennan walks through underwriting a 7-unit turnkey property in Phoenix, demonstrating how to plug actual financials into a pro forma template to identify deal breakeven pricing. He shows that the asking price of $1.825M doesn't work financially, identifies the property would need to trade around $1.6M depending on interest rates, and emphasizes using on-market deal analysis to build broker relationships that lead to off-market opportunities.
takeaways: Smaller multifamily (7-20 units) typically prices at $260k/door in Phoenix vs. $225k/door for larger 25-40 unit deals in the same market
Always add reserves at 5% of expenses and turnover costs (~$1k per unit turn) even if seller doesn't include them
Target minimum 1.25x debt service coverage ratio; loans under $1M get worse rates/terms than Freddie/Fannie loans above $1M
Location analysis: multifamily surrounded by single-family homes or residential zones is ideal; avoid junkyards, industrial, gas stations, and commercial mixed-use
Use realistic interest rate assumptions (6% vs. optimistic 3-4%); a 3% rate change makes massive DCR/return differences on small deals
Target 4-8% cash-on-cash returns and 12-15% IRR on pro forma metrics to have a viable deal
'On to off' strategy: leverage on-market deal analysis and offers to build broker relationships that unlock off-market deals before they hit public listing sites

skill_candidates.change: Learn to construct detailed pro forma with all expense line items (maintenance, turnover, management fee, reserves)
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin shows plugging in missing line items like turnover costs ($1k/turn) and 5% reserves that seller omitted; demonstrates how this changes total expenses from $24k to $27k

skill_candidates.change: Develop ability to back into offer price from target returns rather than accepting seller's list price
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin shows working backward from 4-8% cash-on-cash and 12-15% IRR targets to identify $1.6M is true value despite $1.825M ask, teaching students 'where you need to buy it at'

skill_candidates.change: Use preliminary LOI on inflated-ask deals to initiate broker conversation and credibility-building, not just to negotiate price
skill_candidates.evidence: Student sent LOI at $1.6M on $1.825M ask; broker was shocked but it 'teed up the conversation' and led to broker viewing student as serious/knowledgeable, creating foundation for future off-market deal flow

skill_candidates.change: Apply geographic and market-rate comparables to identify per-unit pricing anomalies ($260k/door vs. $225k/door for larger buildings)
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explicitly teaches 'when you get comfortable with markets, you start to notice these things' and shows 7-unit deals pricing higher per unit than 25-40 unit comps in same Phoenix market

title: Case Study 1 - Underwriting a 8-20 unit deal
